name: Louisville/Jefferson County Metro, Kentucky
tagline: Kentucky's largest local government — the consolidated Louisville/Jefferson County Metro (~770K residents). Central IT runs through Metro Technology Services (MTS), and the city is moving early on enterprise AI under a new Chief AI Officer and a four-person AI office.
overview: Louisville/Jefferson County Metro Government is the consolidated city-county government formed when Louisville and Jefferson County merged — the largest local government in Kentucky, serving roughly 770,000 residents. It is a strong-mayor government led by Mayor Craig Greenberg, with a 26-member Metro Council. Central technology runs through Metro Technology Services (MTS), the enterprise IT organization that delivers infrastructure, networking, applications, public-safety systems, GIS/smart-city, and cybersecurity across Metro departments, led by executive director (CIO) Chris Seidt. For a Dell SLG seller, Louisville Metro is best understood as a single, sizable municipal buyer with centralized IT and an unusually forward AI posture: in December 2025 the city named its first Chief Artificial Intelligence Officer (Pamela McKnight, formerly of Intel) and stood up a small AI office, then in January 2026 awarded its first AI pilot (permitting) via RFP. Cybersecurity has been led inside MTS for roughly a decade, and the city has a long-running smart-city program. Procurement is centralized in the Office of Management & Budget (OMB), and Metro can buy off cooperative vehicles (e.g., Sourcewell, NASPO ValuePoint via the KY participating addendum) in addition to its own competitive solicitations.

budget.intro: Louisville Metro is a direct end spender with a centralized budget, unlike the state cabinets — its leverage is its own ~$1.1B annual budget plus a centralized IT and procurement function. Metro Council approved the amended FY2026–27 (FY27) operating and capital budget of roughly $1.1 billion in June 2026 in a 22–3 vote, with public safety as the largest category and continued investment in housing, paving, and parks. MTS-specific line items were not separately confirmed here; treat IT as funded within the central budget plus dedicated AI dollars.

stats.note: Amended operating + capital, approved June 2026 by a 22–3 Council vote

stats.label: AI initiative expansion
stats.note: Budget expansion tied to the new Chief AI Officer and four-person AI office

stats.note: Kentucky's largest local government; consolidated city-county scale

notes: Council amended the mayor's proposal — trimming the Belvedere renovation and Affordable Housing Trust Fund from $15M to $10M each and raising park maintenance to ~$4.4M — so confirm final line items against the enacted ordinance.
Public safety (incl. LMPD) is the single largest spending category; technology and IT spend sit within the central budget rather than a separately published large IT line.
MTS-specific appropriations were not separately confirmed here — treat the IT/AI split as directional and validate against the published FY27 budget book.

initiatives.name: Enterprise AI office & Chief AI Officer
initiatives.summary: Louisville named its first Chief Artificial Intelligence Officer (Pamela McKnight, ex-Intel) in December 2025 to lead an enterprise-wide AI strategy and a four-person AI team focused on responsible adoption across departments. A clear entry point for AI-infrastructure, responsible-AI, and workstation/compute conversations — engage the AI office and MTS together.

initiatives.name: AI permitting pilot (Govstream.ai)
initiatives.summary: In January 2026 Metro awarded its first AI pilot — via its first AI-focused RFP — to Govstream.ai to streamline residential permitting using assistants grounded in the city's own codes, spatial data, and permit records (aggregate fee cap ~$30K, running through spring 2026). A signal that Metro is moving from policy to procured AI use cases.

initiatives.name: Cybersecurity & compliance
initiatives.summary: MTS has run Metro's cybersecurity, risk-management, compliance, and security-awareness program for roughly a decade (led by James Meece since 2015). Cyber also helped shape the city's AI policy. A standing lane for security, identity, endpoint, and data-protection offerings across a complex municipal estate.

initiatives.name: Smart city & civic innovation
initiatives.summary: Louisville runs a long-standing smart-city / civic-innovation program inside MTS (open data, sensors, GIS, connectivity), with a long-term vision of fiber-to-the-home and environmental sensing. Relevant for edge/IoT data infrastructure, networking, and the compute behind data and analytics.

procurement.intro: Selling to Louisville Metro runs through the Office of Management & Budget (OMB) Procurement Division, whose director is delegated purchasing authority and reports to Metro's CFO. Confirm two things first: which path fits (Metro's own competitive solicitation vs. a cooperative/piggyback contract), and that Dell's cooperative coverage is active and correctly scoped for a Kentucky local buyer.

vehicles.name: Louisville Metro OMB Procurement
vehicles.detail: Central purchasing authority for Metro Government — competitive sealed bids, competitive negotiation, cooperative, piggyback, and sole/single-source under the OMB Procurement Policy & Manual. Supplier registration and active solicitations run through OMB Procurement.

vehicles.name: Cooperative contracts (e.g., Sourcewell)
vehicles.detail: Metro policy expressly allows cooperative purchasing after due diligence; Sourcewell is a commonly used national cooperative. Confirm Dell's relevant cooperative contract is active and that Metro is an eligible/registered member before forecasting.

vehicles.name: Kentucky NASPO ValuePoint addendum
vehicles.detail: As a Kentucky local government, Metro can typically leverage the state's NASPO ValuePoint participating addenda (e.g., the Dell MNWNC-109 computer-equipment master price agreement via the KY addendum). Verify local-buyer eligibility and configuration limits per addendum.

powerStructure.name: Craig Greenberg
powerStructure.title: Mayor, Louisville Metro
powerStructure.note: Strong-mayor executive; AI-forward — personally announced the first Chief AI Officer and the first AI pilot. Top of the house and the sponsor of the city's technology/AI agenda.

powerStructure.name: Chris Seidt
powerStructure.title: Executive Director / CIO, Metro Technology Services (MTS)
powerStructure.note: ~20-year Metro IT veteran (Smart Cities, IT Operations) and executive director since 2018. The primary enterprise IT relationship across infrastructure, apps, and security.

powerStructure.name: Pamela McKnight
powerStructure.title: Chief Artificial Intelligence Officer (CAIO)
powerStructure.note: Named December 2025 from Intel; leads enterprise AI strategy and a four-person AI office. The owner of the AI buying center — engage alongside MTS.

powerStructure.name: James Meece
powerStructure.title: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO), MTS
powerStructure.note: Has led Metro's cybersecurity, risk, and compliance program since 2015 (title reported as CISO in 2026) and helped shape the city's AI policy; Cody Pierce is reported as deputy CISO. The security relationship to build before forecasting cyber deals.

powerStructure.name: OMB Procurement Division
powerStructure.title: Central purchasing (under Metro CFO)
powerStructure.note: Procurement director is delegated buying authority and reports to the CFO within OMB — owns solicitations, cooperative approvals, and supplier onboarding; the contracting path into Metro.

news.title: Louisville drafts data-center zoning rules — would ban hyperscale, cap facilities <500K sq ft
news.impact: The Office of Planning's June 9 draft (public comment through July 9) would restrict data centers to industrial zones, ban hyperscale, and put infrastructure costs on developers — signaling a cautious posture toward large local compute build-out and shaping where AI/data-center demand can land in the metro. Watch how it interacts with Metro's own AI/compute needs.

news.title: Metro Council approves amended ~$1.1B FY27 budget (22–3)
news.impact: Sets the FY27 spending envelope with public safety as the largest category; confirm final IT/AI line items against the enacted ordinance and frame buys around modernization ROI.

news.title: GovTech profiles Louisville's "build-internally" modernization under CIO Seidt
news.impact: A May 26 feature highlights Metro's strategy of building internal technical capacity (private-sector hires paired with IT, a customized Workday ERP, free park Wi-Fi, and a 2026 service-delivery redesign) rather than pure buy-and-deploy — position Dell around infrastructure/lifecycle and as-a-service that augments in-house teams, not replaces them.

news.title: Louisville awards first AI pilot to Govstream.ai for permitting
news.impact: First AI use case procured via RFP (~$30K cap, through spring) — signals Metro is moving from AI policy to procured deployments; an opening to position AI infrastructure and responsible-AI offerings.

news.title: Louisville names first Chief AI Officer (Pamela McKnight)
news.impact: Creates a dedicated AI buying center and a four-person AI office backed by a ~$2M expansion — build the relationship early alongside MTS. (Announced late 2025; formally introduced at a Jan 7, 2026 press conference.)

dellActions: Confirm Dell's access paths for a Kentucky local buyer first — Metro's OMB cooperative/piggyback options (e.g., Sourcewell) and the NASPO ValuePoint KY addendum (MNWNC-109) — and verify eligibility and configuration limits before forecasting.
Build the relationship with MTS executive director / CIO Chris Seidt as the enterprise IT owner across infrastructure, apps, and security.

Engage the new AI office and CAIO Pamela McKnight alongside MTS — position AI infrastructure, responsible-AI, and AI-ready workstations/compute tied to the city's permitting pilot and broader AI roadmap.
Align security pitches to MTS cybersecurity (James Meece) — endpoint, identity, and data protection across a complex municipal estate, referencing the city's AI policy and compliance posture.

Frame buys around the ~$1.1B FY27 budget: lead with consolidation/lifecycle savings, smart-city/data infrastructure, and as-a-service economics, and validate final line items against the enacted budget.
